questions.question:
What is the maximum height of any AVL-tree with 7 nodes? Assume that the height of a tree with a single node is 0.

A. 2
B. 3
C. 4
D. 5
questions.to_find: Maximum height of an AVL tree with 7 nodes (height of single-node tree = 0)

understand.plain: An AVL tree is a balanced BST where for every node, the heights of left and right subtrees differ by at most 1. We want to find the tallest AVL tree that can be built using exactly 7 nodes, where height is defined as the number of edges on the longest path from root to a leaf (single node = height 0).

keywords.explain: A self-balancing BST where the balance factor (|left height - right height|) of every node is at most 1.
keywords.example: A tree with root having left subtree height 2 and right subtree height 1 is valid AVL (difference = 1).

keywords.explain: The number of edges on the longest path from the root to any leaf. A single-node tree has height 0.
keywords.example: A root with one child has height 1.

understand.visual_alt: An AVL tree of height 3 with 7 nodes: root at level 0, 2 nodes at level 1, 3 nodes at level 2, 2 nodes at level 3.

given.aim: Find the maximum height achievable in an AVL tree with exactly 7 nodes.

terms.meaning: Total number of nodes in the AVL tree.
terms.example: 7 nodes to be arranged to maximize height while maintaining AVL property.
terms.connects: More nodes allow taller trees; we want maximum height consistent with AVL balance.

terms.term: N(h) = minimum nodes for AVL tree of height h
terms.meaning: The recurrence N(h) = N(h-1) + N(h-2) + 1, with N(0)=1, N(1)=2.
terms.connects: If N(h) <= 7, then height h is achievable with 7 nodes.

terms.meaning: Difference between left and right subtree heights; must be -1, 0, or +1 for every node.
terms.example: A node with left height 2 and right height 1 has balance factor 1 (valid).
terms.connects: Ensures tree stays balanced and limits maximum height.

given.plan: Use the minimum-node AVL tree recurrence N(h) = N(h-1) + N(h-2) + 1. Find the largest h such that N(h) <= 7. Since a tree with N(h) nodes has height h, and we have 7 nodes available, maximum height h satisfies N(h) <= 7.

steps.title: Compute N(h) values using the recurrence
steps.note: N(h) gives the MINIMUM number of nodes needed for an AVL tree of height h.

steps.title: Find the maximum height with 7 nodes
steps.formula_raw: max height h such that N(h) <= n
steps.apply:
N(3) = 7 <= 7: height 3 is achievable.
N(4) = 12 > 7: height 4 requires at least 12 nodes, not achievable with 7.
Therefore maximum height = 3.
steps.note: With exactly 7 nodes we can construct a maximally-unbalanced (but still AVL-valid) tree of height 3.

solution.result: The maximum height of an AVL tree with 7 nodes is 3. Answer: B. 3

questions.formula_note: Minimum-node AVL tree recurrence: N(h) = N(h-1) + N(h-2) + 1 (similar to Fibonacci). Maximum height with n nodes is floor(log_phi(sqrt(5)*(n+1))) - 2, approximately 1.44 * log_2(n).

questions.question:
What is the worst case time complexity of inserting n elements into an AVL-tree with n elements initially?

A. Theta(n log n)
B. Theta(n^2)
C. Theta(n^2 log n)
D. Theta(n^3)
questions.to_find: Worst-case time complexity of inserting n elements into an AVL tree that already contains n elements.

understand.plain: We start with an AVL tree that already has n elements, and we want to insert n more elements one by one. Each individual insertion into an AVL tree takes O(log n) time in the worst case (because the tree height is O(log n)). We need to find the total time for all n insertions.

keywords.explain: Inserting into an AVL tree takes O(log n) time: O(log n) to find the position + O(log n) to perform rotations to restore balance.
keywords.example: Inserting into an AVL tree with 1000 nodes takes at most about log_2(1000) ~ 10 operations.

keywords.explain: Theta(f(n)) means the running time is both O(f(n)) and Omega(f(n)) — tight bound from above and below.
keywords.example: Theta(n log n) means the time grows exactly like n log n for large n.

understand.visual_alt: Inserting n elements one-by-one into an AVL tree: each insertion costs O(log(2n)) = O(log n), so total = n * O(log n) = O(n log n).

given.aim: Determine the total worst-case time to insert n new elements into an AVL tree that starts with n elements.

terms.meaning: The AVL tree already has n elements before any new insertion.
terms.example: If n=100, the tree has 100 nodes and height at most 1.44 * log_2(100) ~ 10.
terms.connects: Sets the starting height of the tree.

terms.example: Insert elements n+1, n+2, ..., 2n into the tree.

terms.meaning: Each insertion into an AVL tree with k nodes costs O(log k) in the worst case.
terms.example: At any point the tree has between n and 2n nodes, so each insertion costs O(log n).
terms.connects: Summing n insertions each of O(log n) gives total O(n log n).

given.plan: Each insertion into an AVL tree costs O(log(current size)). The tree grows from n to 2n nodes. At every step the tree has at most 2n nodes, so each insertion costs O(log(2n)) = O(log n). Total cost = n * O(log n) = O(n log n). Since each insertion also costs Omega(log n), the total is Theta(n log n).

steps.title: Cost of a single insertion into an AVL tree
steps.formula_raw: Single insertion cost = O(log k) where k = current tree size
steps.apply:
AVL tree of k nodes has height at most 1.44 * log_2(k) = O(log k).
Insertion requires traversal down to a leaf: O(log k).
Rebalancing via rotations: O(log k) rotations in worst case, each O(1).
Total per insertion: O(log k).
steps.note: AVL rotations after insertion: at most O(log n) rotations travel up the tree.

steps.title: Sum the cost for n insertions
steps.apply:
When inserting the i-th new element, the tree has n+i-1 nodes.
Cost = O(log(n+i-1)) <= O(log(2n)) = O(log n) for all i from 1 to n.
Total = sum_{i=1}^{n} O(log n) = n * O(log n) = O(n log n).
Since each insertion costs Omega(log n), total = Theta(n log n).
steps.note: The initial n elements do not change; we only add n more, so the tree size stays O(n) throughout.

solution.result: Worst-case time complexity is Theta(n log n). Answer: A. Theta(n log n)

questions.formula_note: AVL tree insertion: O(log n) per operation (height is O(log n)). Total for n insertions: Theta(n log n).

questions.question:
Which of the following is TRUE?

A. The cost of searching an AVL tree is Theta(log n) but that of a binary search tree is O(n)
B. The cost of searching an AVL tree is Theta(log n) but that of a complete binary tree is Theta(n log n)
C. The cost of searching a binary search tree is O(log n) but that of an AVL tree is Theta(n)
D. The cost of searching an AVL tree is Theta(n log n) but that of a binary search tree is O(n)
questions.answer: A. The cost of searching an AVL tree is Theta(log n) but that of a binary search tree is O(n)
questions.to_find: The true statement about search costs in AVL trees vs. binary search trees.

understand.plain: We need to compare the search complexity of AVL trees and ordinary binary search trees (BSTs). AVL trees are always height-balanced (height = O(log n)), so search is always Theta(log n). A plain BST can degenerate into a linked list (height = O(n)) in the worst case, so search is O(n) worst case.

keywords.explain: Always Theta(log n) because the AVL property guarantees height is at most 1.44 * log_2(n).
keywords.example: Searching in an AVL tree of 1 million nodes takes at most ~29 comparisons.

keywords.explain: Worst case O(n) because a BST may be completely unbalanced (e.g., inserting sorted data creates a linear chain).
keywords.example: Inserting 1,2,3,...,n into a BST creates a right-skewed tree of height n-1; searching can take O(n).

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 440 200' width='440' height='200'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><text x='110' y='25' class='tag ink' text-anchor='middle'>AVL Tree</text><text x='330' y='25' class='tag ink' text-anchor='middle'>Unbalanced BST</text><circle cx='110' cy='55' r='15' class='hi'/><line x1='110' y1='70' x2='75' y2='100' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='110' y1='70' x2='145' y2='100' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><circle cx='75' cy='115' r='15' class='hi'/><circle cx='145' cy='115' r='15' class='hi'/><line x1='75' y1='130' x2='60' y2='160' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='75' y1='130' x2='90' y2='160' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><circle cx='60' cy='175' r='15' class='hi'/><circle cx='90' cy='175' r='15' class='hi'/><text x='110' y='195' class='sub ok' text-anchor='middle'>Height = O(log n)</text><circle cx='290' cy='55' r='15' class='bg'/><line x1='290' y1='70' x2='330' y2='100' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><circle cx='330' cy='115' r='15' class='bg'/><line x1='330' y1='130' x2='370' y2='160' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><circle cx='370' cy='175' r='15' class='bg'/><text x='330' y='195' class='sub no' text-anchor='middle'>Height = O(n) worst case</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Left: balanced AVL tree with height O(log n). Right: skewed BST (right-linear chain) with height O(n).

given.aim: Identify the correct statement about search time complexity in AVL trees and BSTs.

terms.meaning: Always Theta(log n) due to the balance invariant.
terms.example: An AVL tree with n=1000 nodes has height at most 14.
terms.connects: Search traverses root-to-leaf, so cost = Theta(log n).

terms.meaning: A BST can have height O(n) if elements are inserted in sorted or reverse-sorted order.
terms.example: Inserting 1,2,3,4,5 into BST creates a right-skewed tree; search for 5 takes 5 steps.
terms.connects: Worst-case search in BST is O(n).

terms.meaning: A complete binary tree has height Theta(log n), so search is Theta(log n), not Theta(n log n).
terms.example: A full complete binary tree with n nodes has all leaves at depth floor(log_2 n).
terms.connects: Option B is wrong because it claims complete binary tree search is Theta(n log n).

given.plan: Check each option: AVL search = Theta(log n) (always balanced). BST search = O(n) worst case (can be skewed). Complete binary tree search = Theta(log n) (always balanced). Use these facts to identify the true statement.

steps.title: Analyze search cost in AVL trees
steps.apply: AVL trees maintain the invariant that left and right subtrees differ in height by at most 1. This guarantees the tree height is always Theta(log n). Therefore, search in an AVL tree always takes Theta(log n) time.
steps.note: This is the key advantage of AVL trees over plain BSTs.

steps.title: Analyze search cost in BSTs and evaluate each option
steps.apply:
Option A: AVL = Theta(log n), BST = O(n). Both correct. TRUE.
Option B: Complete binary tree search = Theta(log n), not Theta(n log n). FALSE.
Option C: Claims AVL search = Theta(n), BST = O(log n). Both wrong. FALSE.
Option D: Claims AVL search = Theta(n log n). Wrong. FALSE.
steps.note: A plain BST on sorted input degenerates to a linked list of height n-1, giving O(n) worst-case search.

solution.result: Option A is TRUE: AVL tree search is Theta(log n); BST search is O(n) in the worst case.

questions.formula_note: AVL tree height is always Theta(log n) due to the balance factor invariant. Unbalanced BST can have height O(n) in the worst case.

questions.question:
The following Pascal program segment finds the largest number in a two-dimensional integer array A[0..n-1, 0..n-1] using a single loop. Fill up the boxes to complete the program.

Assume that max is a variable to store the largest value and i, j are the indices to the array.

begin
  max := A[0, 0];
  for k := 1 to ___[A] do
    begin
      i := k div n;
      j := k mod n;
      if A[i, j] > max then max := A[i, j]
    end
end

What is the correct expression for box [A]?

A. n^2 - 1
B. n^2
C. n - 1
D. 2n - 1
questions.to_find: The correct upper bound for the loop variable k to traverse all elements of an n x n 2D array using a single loop.

understand.plain: A 2D array A[0..n-1, 0..n-1] has n*n = n^2 elements. We want to visit all elements using a single loop variable k. We map k to row i = k div n and column j = k mod n. The loop should run from k=1 to n^2-1 (since k=0 is already handled as the initial max = A[0,0]).

keywords.explain: Mapping a 2D index (i,j) to a single index k = i*n + j, so we can traverse all elements with one loop.
keywords.example: For n=3, element A[1][2] maps to k = 1*3+2 = 5.

keywords.explain: Integer division and remainder: i = k div n gives the row, j = k mod n gives the column.

understand.visual_alt: A 2D array traversal: element (i,j) is accessed as k = i*n+j. For an n x n array, k ranges from 0 to n^2-1.

given.aim: Determine the upper bound of the loop variable k to cover all n^2 elements of an n x n array, given k=0 is already processed.

terms.meaning: A 2D array with n rows and n columns, total n^2 elements.
terms.example: For n=3: indices from A[0,0] to A[2,2], total 9 elements.
terms.connects: Total elements to visit determines the loop bound.

terms.meaning: max is initialized to A[0,0] which corresponds to k=0. The loop starts at k=1.
terms.example: i = 0 div n = 0, j = 0 mod n = 0, so k=0 maps to A[0,0].
terms.connects: Loop runs from k=1, so must end at n^2-1 to cover all n^2 elements.

terms.meaning: The last element A[n-1, n-1] maps to k = (n-1)*n + (n-1) = n^2 - 1.
terms.example: For n=3: last element A[2,2] has k = 2*3+2 = 8 = 3^2-1.
terms.connects: Loop upper bound must be n^2-1.

given.plan: The n x n array has n^2 elements indexed k=0 to n^2-1. k=0 is handled before the loop (max := A[0,0]). The loop runs from k=1 to n^2-1, covering the remaining n^2-1 elements. So box [A] = n^2-1.

steps.title: Determine total elements and linear index range
steps.formula_raw: Total elements = n * n = n^2; k ranges from 0 to n^2-1
steps.apply:
An n x n array has n^2 elements.
Linear index k maps (i,j) to k = i*n + j.
For i=0,j=0: k=0 (min). For i=n-1,j=n-1: k=(n-1)*n+(n-1) = n^2-n+n-1 = n^2-1 (max).
So k ranges from 0 to n^2-1.
steps.note: This linearization visits elements in row-major order.

steps.title: Set the loop bound
steps.apply:
The program initializes max := A[0,0] (k=0 handled separately).
The loop must visit k=1, 2, ..., n^2-1 to cover all remaining elements.
Therefore box [A] = n^2 - 1.
steps.note: If box [A] were n^2, the loop would try to access A[n, 0] which is out of bounds for a 0-indexed array.

questions.formula_note: Row-major linearization: element (i,j) in an n-column array maps to linear index k = i*n + j. For an n x n array, k ranges from 0 to n^2-1.

questions.question:
In a compact single dimensional array representation for lower triangular matrices (i.e., all elements above the diagonal are zero) of size n x n, non-zero elements (i.e., elements of the lower triangle) of each row are stored one after another, starting from the first row, the index of the (i, j)th element of the lower triangular matrix in this new representation is:

A. i + j - 1
B. i(i-1)/2 + j
C. (j-1) + i
D. i + j^(3/2)
questions.to_find: The index of the (i,j)th element in a compact 1D array representation of a lower triangular matrix (1-indexed).

understand.plain: A lower triangular matrix stores only elements where row >= column (the rest are zero). We pack these non-zero elements row by row into a 1D array. Row 1 has 1 element, row 2 has 2 elements, ..., row i has i elements. The (i,j)th element (1-indexed, j <= i) is stored after all elements of rows 1 through i-1, then at position j within row i.

keywords.explain: An n x n matrix where all elements above the main diagonal are zero; element (i,j) is non-zero only when j <= i.
keywords.example: A 3x3 lower triangular matrix has 6 non-zero elements: (1,1),(2,1),(2,2),(3,1),(3,2),(3,3).

keywords.explain: Store only the non-zero elements in a 1D array row by row, saving space.
keywords.example: Row 1: 1 element, row 2: 2 elements, row 3: 3 elements. Total: 1+2+3=6 for n=3.

understand.visual_alt: A 4x4 lower triangular matrix (highlighted cells) mapped to a 1D array row by row: a11 at index 1, a21 at 2, a22 at 3, a31 at 4, a32 at 5, a33 at 6, etc.

given.aim: Find the 1D array index of element A[i][j] (1-indexed, j <= i) in the compact lower triangular storage.

terms.meaning: Row 1 has 1 element, row 2 has 2, ..., row i-1 has i-1 elements. Total = 1+2+...+(i-1) = i(i-1)/2.
terms.example: Before row 3 (i=3): rows 1 and 2 have 1+2=3 elements stored.
terms.connects: These elements occupy positions 1 through i(i-1)/2 in the 1D array.

terms.term: Position of A[i][j] within row i
terms.meaning: Within row i, element A[i][j] is the j-th element (since j ranges 1..i).
terms.example: A[3][2] is the 2nd element in row 3.
terms.connects: Adds j to the offset from previous rows.

terms.meaning: Index = (elements in rows 1..i-1) + j = i(i-1)/2 + j.
terms.connects: This is the complete formula for the compact storage index.

given.plan: Count elements stored before row i: sum = 1+2+...+(i-1) = i(i-1)/2. Then the j-th element of row i is at index i(i-1)/2 + j.

steps.title: Count elements stored before row i
steps.formula_raw: Elements in rows 1 to i-1 = 1 + 2 + ... + (i-1) = i(i-1)/2
steps.apply:
Row 1 stores 1 element.
Row 2 stores 2 elements.
...
Row i-1 stores i-1 elements.
Total = sum_{k=1}^{i-1} k = (i-1)*i/2 = i(i-1)/2.
These fill positions 1 through i(i-1)/2 in the 1D array.
steps.note: Using the arithmetic series formula: sum of first m integers = m(m+1)/2, with m = i-1.

steps.title: Compute the index of A[i][j]
steps.apply:
Element A[i][j] is the j-th element in row i.
Index = (elements before row i) + j = i(i-1)/2 + j.

Verification:
A[1][1]: 1*0/2 + 1 = 1. (First element stored.) Correct.
A[2][1]: 2*1/2 + 1 = 2. (Second element stored.) Correct.
A[2][2]: 2*1/2 + 2 = 3. (Third element stored.) Correct.
A[3][2]: 3*2/2 + 2 = 5. (Fifth element stored.) Correct.
steps.note: This formula works for 1-based indexing where i is the row (1 <= j <= i <= n).

solution.result: The index of A[i][j] in the compact lower triangular array is i(i-1)/2 + j. Answer: B. i(i-1)/2 + j

questions.formula_note: Lower triangular matrix compact storage: index(i,j) = i(i-1)/2 + j (1-based). Uses arithmetic series sum 1+2+...+(i-1) = i(i-1)/2 to count preceding elements.
